title: Craniosynostosis: Recognition and Management
objectives: Identify skull deformities associated with single-suture synostosis

sections.title: Pathophysiology
sections.content:
**Normal Sutures:**
• Allow brain growth perpendicular to suture
• Fuse by age 2-3 years
• Metopic closes first (~8 months)

**Craniosynostosis:**
• Premature fusion of one or more sutures
• Brain grows parallel to fused suture
• Compensatory growth at open sutures

**Virchow's Law:**
Growth restriction perpendicular to fused suture
Compensatory growth parallel to fused suture

**Incidence:** 1/2,000-2,500 births

sections.title: Single Suture Types
sections.content:
**Sagittal (Most common - 50%):**
• Long, narrow skull (scaphocephaly/dolichocephaly)
• Frontal and occipital bossing
• Palpable ridge along sagittal suture

**Metopic (15-20%):**
• Triangular forehead (trigonocephaly)
• Hypotelorism
• Palpable midline frontal ridge

**Coronal (20-25%):**
• Unilateral: Anterior plagiocephaly
• Bilateral: Brachycephaly
• Harlequin eye deformity

**Lambdoid (Rare - 2-4%):**
• Posterior plagiocephaly
• Ipsilateral ear displaced inferiorly
• Trapezoid skull from above

sections.title: Diagnosis
sections.content:
**Clinical Evaluation:**
• Head shape from above (vertex view)
• Palpation for ridges
• Facial asymmetry
• Ear position
• Head circumference

**Key Differentiation - Plagiocephaly:**
• Positional: Parallelogram shape, anterior ear shift
• Lambdoid synostosis: Trapezoid shape, posterior ear shift

**Imaging:**
• 3D CT scan: Gold standard
• Skull X-rays: Limited utility
• May see 'beaten copper' if ICP elevated

**Ophthalmology:**
• Fundoscopic exam for papilledema
• Especially in syndromic cases

sections.title: Surgical Management
sections.content:
**Goals:**
• Allow normal brain growth
• Correct cosmetic deformity
• Prevent elevated ICP

**Timing:**
• Optimal: 3-6 months for endoscopic
• Open: 6-12 months
• Earlier = better brain remodeling

**Techniques:**

**Endoscopic Strip Craniectomy:**
• Minimally invasive
• <4 months old
• Requires helmet therapy (6-12 months)

**Open Cranial Vault Remodeling:**
• Fronto-orbital advancement
• Posterior vault distraction
• Pi plasty, barrel stave osteotomies
• No helmet required

sections.title: Syndromic Craniosynostosis
sections.content:
**Crouzon Syndrome:**
• Bicoronal synostosis
• Midface hypoplasia
• Proptosis
• Normal intelligence and extremities
• FGFR2 mutation

**Apert Syndrome:**
• Bicoronal synostosis
• Midface hypoplasia
• Syndactyly (mitten hands/feet)
• Often intellectual disability
• FGFR2 mutation

**Pfeiffer Syndrome:**
• Variable suture involvement
• Broad thumbs and great toes
• Type II/III have cloverleaf skull

**Muenke Syndrome:**
• Coronal synostosis
• FGFR3 mutation
• Carpal/tarsal coalition

sections.title: Outcomes & Complications
sections.content:
**ICP Concerns:**
• Multi-suture synostosis: 30-40% risk
• Single suture: 15-20% risk
• Monitor for headaches, papilledema

**Surgical Complications:**
• Blood loss (significant in infants)
• CSF leak
• Infection
• Need for revision

**Long-term:**
• Most achieve good cosmetic outcomes
• Syndromic cases need multiple surgeries
• Developmental monitoring important

**Follow-up:**
• Head circumference
• Ophthalmology
• Neuropsychological if syndromic

keyTakeaways: Sagittal synostosis is most common → scaphocephaly
Use vertex view to assess head shape
Differentiate from positional plagiocephaly by ear position
Early surgery (3-6 months) allows endoscopic approach
Syndromic forms have multi-suture involvement and require genetics evaluation
Monitor for elevated ICP, especially in multi-suture cases

reviewQuestions.question: A 4-month-old has a long, narrow head with frontal and occipital bossing. A ridge is palpable along the midline of the skull. What suture is most likely affected?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Sagittal synostosis produces scaphocephaly (boat-shaped skull) - long and narrow with frontal and occipital bossing. The palpable midline ridge confirms sagittal suture involvement. This is the most common single-suture craniosynostosis.
